food_subgroup: Mixed vegetable salad with fermented tea leaves and corn

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese tea leaf salad with corn. FOODON_03301710 (salad food product) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for mixed vegetable salads used as closest parent; no specific code exists for laphet thoke or Burmese tea leaf salad.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese tea leaf salad or laphet thoke. SNOMED CT does not cover this dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g (one plate), as typically served at a Singaporean café or Myanmar restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Laphet Thoke (Pickled Tea Leaf Salad) — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central entries for corn (sweet corn, yellow, raw, FDC ID 169998), peanuts (FDC ID 172430), sesame seeds (FDC ID 169416), and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for dried shrimp and fish sauce; fermented tea leaf composition referenced from peer-reviewed literature (Khaing et al., 2019, Journal of Food Composition and Analysis).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; peer-reviewed literature
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for corn tea leaf salad (laphet thoke with corn) exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 40% sweet corn kernels, 15% pickled tea leaves, 15% peanuts, 10% tomato, 5% sesame seeds, 5% dried shrimp, 5% fried garlic, and 5% dressing (lime juice, fish sauce). Values are cross-referenced against general Burmese salad composition data from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet corn and mixed salad analogue — estimated from sweet corn GI (GI=52, Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) and mixed vegetable salad components; weighted estimate applied for composite dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for corn tea leaf salad (laphet thoke with corn). GI estimated at 52 based on sweet corn as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate contributor (GI=52 per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008). Pickled tea leaves, peanuts, sesame, tomato, and dressing components contribute negligible glycemic load. Composite GI weighted toward sweet corn. GL per serving = (52 × 13.5 × 200/100) / 100 = 14.04, rounded to 14.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, primarily from fish sauce and dried shrimp, which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive patients; requesting reduced fish sauce or omitting dried shrimp can lower sodium by an estimated 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index from sweet corn, moderated by fibre, fat, and protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 (low category) and a glycemic load of approximately 14 per 200 g serving, corn tea leaf salad is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as the fibre from corn and tea leaves (approximately 5.6 g per serving) and the fat and protein from peanuts and sesame seeds slow glucose absorption and blunt postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame seeds), which is cardioprotective; however, the elevated sodium content (~840 mg per serving) warrants caution for patients with cardiovascular disease who are on sodium-restricted diets, and portion control or sodium reduction modifications are advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Corn Tea Leaf Salad (ပြောင်းဖူးလက်ဖက်သုပ်, romanized: Pyaung-zu Laphet Thoke) is a Burmese-origin salad dish that combines sweet corn kernels with pickled fermented tea leaves (laphet), fried peanuts, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, tomato, fried garlic, lime juice, fish sauce, and green chilli. It is a variation of the iconic Burmese laphet thoke (tea leaf salad), which is considered Myanmar's national dish and a culturally significant food served at social gatherings, celebrations, and as an everyday snack or side dish. The addition of sweet corn gives the salad a mild sweetness and additional texture that balances the umami-rich, slightly bitter fermented tea leaves. The dish is tossed rather than cooked, making it a minimally processed salad. The texture is complex: crunchy from fried garlic and peanuts, chewy from the tea leaves, juicy from tomato, and tender from the corn. The flavour profile is simultaneously sour (lime), salty (fish sauce, dried shrimp), umami (laphet, dried shrimp), nutty (peanuts, sesame), and mildly spicy (green chilli). Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (~6.2 g per 100 g) from peanuts, sesame, and dried shrimp, and a favourable unsaturated fat profile. Dietary fibre (~2.8 g per 100 g) from corn, tea leaves, and vegetables supports digestive health. The primary clinical concern is sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp, which is relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. In Singapore, this dish is served at Burmese and Myanmar-cuisine restaurants and cafés, including The Light Cafe at High Street Plaza, where it appears on the menu as a fusion or specialty salad item.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for corn tea leaf salad or laphet thoke with corn exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for individual ingredients (sweet corn, peanuts, sesame seeds) and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for dried shrimp and fish sauce. GI is estimated from the sweet corn GI value (52) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine correction: The input JSON listed cuisine as 'Italian' with tags including 'Pizza' and 'Coffee', which is clearly erroneous for a Burmese dish. The cuisine has been corrected to 'Burmese / Myanmar' based on the dish name and Burmese script in the food_name field. The restaurant 'The Light Cafe - High Street Plaza' in Singapore appears to serve a multicuisine menu including Burmese items. 2. Nutrient estimation: All per_100g values are estimated from component analysis as no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a future approved-source entry if one becomes available. 3. Sentinel values: vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used (USDA FoodData Central does not report vitamin D for this dish composition, and FAO/INFOODS SEA does not provide vitamin D for the relevant components). selenium_mcg is set to -1 as selenium content is not reported in the primary component sources used for this estimation. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as the dish contains no hydrogenated oils or industrially produced trans fats; this is confirmed zero by ingredient composition. 4. Halal status: The dish contains dried shrimp and fish sauce; halal status is not confirmed as the sourcing of these ingredients is unknown. 5. GI calculation: GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 13.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 14.04, rounded to 14.1. 6. Multilingual names: Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are provided as this dish is served in Singapore, following the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement. The Burmese script name (ပြောင်းဖူးလက်ဖက်သုပ်) is preserved from the original input and included in aliases.

Estimated all nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central component analysis and FAO/INFOODS SEA data.
